steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Boundaries (punctuation) question testing the complex series rule. The sentence lists three behavioral economists — Ai Hisano, George Akerlof, and Iris Bohnet — each followed by an appositive that already contains a comma (e.g., “a senior lecturer at Kyoto University”). The blank falls between Hisano's identifier and Akerlof's name, which is the boundary between the first and second items in this comma-containing list.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘University, George Akerlof,’: Using a comma to separate series items that already contain commas violates the complex series rule — the reader cannot distinguish item boundaries from appositive commas. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘University George Akerlof,’: No punctuation between “University” and “George Akerlof” creates a run-on within the series; Akerlof's name runs directly into Hisano's affiliation with no boundary marker. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘University; George Akerlof,’: A semicolon correctly separates the first series item (Hisano and her appositive) from the second (Akerlof and his appositive) when items contain internal commas. The comma after “Akerlof” then correctly marks his appositive — professor at Georgetown University — before the next semicolon separating him from Bohnet. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘University; George Akerlof;’: Placing a semicolon after “Akerlof” instead of a comma severs his name from his own appositive — “a professor at Georgetown University” — turning Akerlof into a standalone item with no identifying information attached. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
